the key changes the output"},"test_Avalanche_MessageBitFlip()":{"notice":"1-bit change in the message changes the output"},"test_CrossVal_BothLong()":{"notice":"Both key and message non-trivially long"},"test_CrossVal_LongKeyEmptyMsg()":{"notice":"65-byte key (forces hashing) plus empty message (forces empty inner concat path)"},"test_CrossVal_LongMessage()":{"notice":"Single-byte key, very long message (1024 bytes, 16 SHA-256 blocks)"},"test_CrossVal_Sequential()":{"notice":"Sequential bytes for both key and message"},"test_Determinism()":{"notice":"Same inputs must produce same output across multiple calls"},"test_Distinctness_DifferentKeys()":{"notice":"Different keys with same message produce different outputs"},"test_Distinctness_DifferentMessages()":{"notice":"Different messages with same key produce different outputs"},"test_KeyBoundary_Length63()":{"notice":"Key exactly 63 bytes (just under block size \u2014 should NOT be hashed)"},"test_KeyBoundary_Length64()":{"notice":"Key exactly 64 bytes (block size \u2014 should NOT be hashed; condition is `>` not `>=`)"},"test_KeyBoundary_Length65()":{"notice":"Key exactly 65 bytes (just over block size \u2014 should be hashed)"},"test_MsgBoundary_Empty()":{"notice":"Empty message"},"test_MsgBoundary_Length64()":{"notice":"Message exactly 64 bytes (one SHA-256 internal block)"},"test_MsgBoundary_Length65()":{"notice":"Message exactly 65 bytes (just past one SHA-256 internal block)"},"test_MsgBoundary_OneByte()":{"notice":"Single-byte message"},"test_RFC4231_Case1_ShortKey()":{"notice":"RFC 4231 Test Case 1: short key (20 bytes of 0x0b), data \"Hi There\""},"test_RFC4231_Case2_AsciiKey()":{"notice":"RFC 4231 Test Case 2: ASCII key \"Jefe\""},"test_RFC4231_Case3_LongData()":{"notice":"RFC 4231 Test Case 3: 20-byte key, 50-byte message"},"test_RFC4231_Case4_SequentialKey()":{"notice":"RFC 4231 Test Case 4: 25-byte sequential key, 50-byte message"},"test_RFC4231_Case6_OversizedKeyShortMsg()":{"notice":"RFC 4231 Test Case 6: oversized key (131 bytes), short message"},"test_RFC4231_Case7_OversizedKey()":{"notice":"RFC 4231 Test Case 7: oversized key (131 bytes), long message"},"test_Regression_BlockSizeIs64()":{"notice":"REGRESSION: wrong block size (e.g. 32 instead of 64) SHA-256's block size is 64. If implementation uses 32, the long-key path triggers at the wrong threshold, AND the pad arrays are wrong size. This test would fail in two ways simultaneously if BLOCK_SIZE were 32."},"test_Regression_ConcatenationOrder()":{"notice":"REGRESSION: concatenation order swapped (message before pad) HMAC concatenates pad first, then message/innerHash. If reversed, outputs will not match any RFC vector. Implicitly covered by RFC tests."},"test_Regression_IPadOPadNotSwapped()":{"notice":"REGRESSION: ipad/opad swapped If 0x36 and 0x5c are accidentally swapped, all RFC vectors will fail because the construction is asymmetric. Covered indirectly by RFC tests, but we make it explicit here."},"test_Regression_LongKeyHashed()":{"notice":"REGRESSION: long key not hashed before use If the implementation forgets to hash keys longer than 64 bytes, RFC Case 6 and Case 7 will fail. The test_KeyBoundary_Length65 test is also a stress test for this \u2014 hashing a 65-byte key versus passing it through must produce different outputs."},"test_Regression_ShortKeyZeroPadded()":{"notice":"REGRESSION: short key not zero-padded to 64 bytes If the key isn't padded with zeros, a short key would XOR fewer bytes against the pad, and the resulting inner/outer pads would differ from spec. RFC Case 2 (4-byte key \"Jefe\") would catch this; we make it explicit here for documentation."}},"notice":"Validates HMAC-SHA256 implementation against multiple categories         of test inputs:         - All seven RFC 4231 test vectors (canonical correctness)         - Key-length boundary conditions (off-by-one in normalization)         - Message-length boundary conditions (SHA-256 block edges)         - Determinism (same inputs produce same output)         - Sensitivity / avalanche (1-bit input changes produce           completely different outputs)         - Distinctness (different keys/messages produce different outputs)         - Cross-validation against Python's hmac library reference           implementation on inputs of our choosing         - Regression tests for the five common HMAC returns.excludedArtifacts_: The list of excluded artifact identifiers.

returns.excludedContracts_: The list of excluded contract addresses.

returns.excludedSelectors_: The list of excluded selector configurations.

returns.excludedSenders_: The list of excluded sender addresses.

returns._0: True if any assertion has failed, false otherwise.

returns.targetedArtifactSelectors_: The list of targeted artifact-selector configurations.

returns.targetedArtifacts_: The list of targeted artifact identifiers.

returns.targetedContracts_: The list of targeted contract addresses.

returns.targetedInterfaces_: The list of targeted address-interface configurations.

returns.targetedSelectors_: The list of targeted selector configurations.

returns.targetedSenders_: The list of targeted sender addresses.

failed().notice: Returns true if any test assertion has failed.

targetSenders().notice: Returns sender allowlist configured via `targetSender` (empty means no sender allowlist).

test_Avalanche_KeyBitFlip().notice: 1-bit change in the key changes the output

test_Avalanche_MessageBitFlip().notice: 1-bit change in the message changes the output

test_CrossVal_BothLong().notice: Both key and message non-trivially long

test_CrossVal_LongKeyEmptyMsg().notice: 65-byte key (forces hashing) plus empty message (forces empty inner concat path)

test_CrossVal_LongMessage().notice: Single-byte key, very long message (1024 bytes, 16 SHA-256 blocks)

test_CrossVal_Sequential().notice: Sequential bytes for both key and message

test_Determinism().notice: Same inputs must produce same output across multiple calls

test_Distinctness_DifferentKeys().notice: Different keys with same message produce different outputs

test_Distinctness_DifferentMessages().notice: Different messages with same key produce different outputs

test_KeyBoundary_Length63().notice: Key exactly 63 bytes (just under block size — should NOT be hashed)

test_KeyBoundary_Length64().notice: Key exactly 64 bytes (block size — should NOT be hashed; condition is `>` not `>=`)

test_KeyBoundary_Length65().notice: Key exactly 65 bytes (just over block size — should be hashed)

test_MsgBoundary_Length64().notice: Message exactly 64 bytes (one SHA-256 internal block)

test_MsgBoundary_Length65().notice: Message exactly 65 bytes (just past one SHA-256 internal block)

test_RFC4231_Case1_ShortKey().notice: RFC 4231 Test Case 1: short key (20 bytes of 0x0b), data "Hi There"

test_RFC4231_Case4_SequentialKey().notice: RFC 4231 Test Case 4: 25-byte sequential key, 50-byte message

test_RFC4231_Case6_OversizedKeyShortMsg(.notice: RFC 4231 Test Case 6: oversized key (131 bytes), short message

test_RFC4231_Case7_OversizedKey().notice: RFC 4231 Test Case 7: oversized key (131 bytes), long message

test_Regression_BlockSizeIs64().notice: REGRESSION: wrong block size (e.g. 32 instead of 64) SHA-256's block size is 64. If implementation uses 32, the long-key path triggers at the wrong threshold, AND the pad arrays are wrong size. This test would fail in two ways simultaneously if BLOCK_SIZE were 32.

test_Regression_ConcatenationOrder().notice: REGRESSION: concatenation order swapped (message before pad) HMAC concatenates pad first, then message/innerHash. If reversed, outputs will not match any RFC vector. Implicitly covered by RFC tests.

test_Regression_IPadOPadNotSwapped().notice: REGRESSION: ipad/opad swapped If 0x36 and 0x5c are accidentally swapped, all RFC vectors will fail because the construction is asymmetric. Covered indirectly by RFC tests, but we make it explicit here.

test_Regression_LongKeyHashed().notice: REGRESSION: long key not hashed before use If the implementation forgets to hash keys longer than 64 bytes, RFC Case 6 and Case 7 will fail. The test_KeyBoundary_Length65 test is also a stress test for this — hashing a 65-byte key versus passing it through must produce different outputs.

test_Regression_ShortKeyZeroPadded().notice: REGRESSION: short key not zero-padded to 64 bytes If the key isn't padded with zeros, a short key would XOR fewer bytes against the pad, and the resulting inner/outer pads would differ from spec. RFC Case 2 (4-byte key "Jefe") would catch this; we make it explicit here for documentation.
